description: Squatina squatina, known as the angelshark, is a species of shark in the family Squatinidae (known generally also as angel sharks), that were once widespread in the coastal waters of the northeastern Atlantic Ocean. Well-adapted for camouflaging itself on the sea floor, the angelshark has a flattened form with enlarged pectoral and pelvic fins, giving it a superficial resemblance to a ray.
funFact: The Angelshark was once so abundant in the North Sea that it supported commercial fisheries — it has since been virtually wiped out across its entire northern range and now survives mainly around the Canary Islands, making it the first European shark to be classified as Critically Endangered.
habitat: The angelshark occurs in the temperate waters of the northeastern Atlantic, from southern Norway and Sweden to the Western Sahara and the Canary Islands, including around Ireland and Britain and in the Mediterranean.
diet: An ambush predator that lies motionless and camouflaged on sandy or muddy seabeds, lunging upward to engulf prey with remarkable speed. Feeds primarily on flatfish (sole, plaice, turbot), skates, crustaceans, cephalopods, and small bony fish that venture near the seafloor.
size: Typically 1.5–1.8 m (4.9–5.9 ft); max confirmed ~2.4 m (7.9 ft). Females grow larger than males.

vitalSigns.label: Estimated Population
vitalSigns.value: Unknown — functionally extinct across most of historic range; viable populations only in Canary Islands and scattered Atlantic-European sites

vitalSigns.label: Reproductive Rate
vitalSigns.value: 7–25 pups per litter; ~8–10 month gestation; typically breeds every 2 years

vitalSigns.label: Top Speed
vitalSigns.value: ~10 km/h (~6 mph) cruising; strike lunge occurs in under 0.1 seconds

vitalSigns.label: Diet
vitalSigns.value: Flatfish, skates, crustaceans, cephalopods, small demersal fish

vitalSigns.label: Population Growth Rate
vitalSigns.value: Very low — late maturity, small litters, and biennial breeding cycle severely limit recovery potential

vitalSigns.label: Key Adaptations
vitalSigns.value: Highly flattened body with cryptic patterning for benthic camouflage; rapid upward strike to capture prey; spiracles on top of head allow breathing while buried in sediment

habitatStats.label: Global Range
habitatStats.value: Historically NE Atlantic from southern Norway to Western Sahara, including British Isles, Iberian Peninsula, Mediterranean, and Canary Islands; now functionally confined to Canary Islands and scattered coastal sites

habitatStats.label: Depth Range
habitatStats.value: 0–150 m typical (recorded to ~500 m); strongly associated with shallow sandy and muddy coastal habitats, estuaries, and bays

threats.name: Bycatch
threats.description: The primary ongoing driver of mortality. Caught incidentally in demersal trawls, bottom-set gillnets, and longlines throughout its remaining range. Its benthic lifestyle and shallow coastal habitat overlap heavily with commercial fishing effort. Discard survival is poor due to barotrauma and injury from net handling.

threats.name: Habitat Degradation
threats.description: Depends on shallow coastal sandy and muddy habitats that are disproportionately impacted by coastal development, seabed disturbance from trawling, anchor damage, and marine tourism. Loss and degradation of nursery habitat further constrains the already minimal recruitment.

threats.name: Slow Reproductive Rate
threats.description: Late sexual maturity, small litter sizes, and a biennial reproductive cycle mean that even low levels of ongoing mortality can prevent population recovery. Modelling suggests the species may require decades with near-zero fishing mortality to begin rebuilding.

threats.name: Historical Overfishing
threats.description: Targeted in commercial fisheries across the NE Atlantic and Mediterranean through most of the 20th century for meat and oil. Industrial bottom trawling post-WWII caused rapid range-wide collapse before any management measures were implemented.

threats.name: Climate Change
threats.description: As a cool-temperate specialist largely confined to shallow coastal habitats, the angelshark faces progressive range compression from warming seas and is vulnerable to disruption of prey availability and benthic habitat quality.

actionItems.title: Support the Angel Shark Project
actionItems.description: The Angel Shark Project — coordinated by the Zoological Society of London and partners in the Canary Islands — monitors the last stronghold population, conducts tagging research, and engages local fishers. Donations directly fund field surveys and bycatch mitigation trials.

actionItems.title: Advocate for Bycatch Reduction Measures
actionItems.description: Push fishing authorities and the EU to mandate bycatch mitigation devices in demersal trawl and gillnet fisheries within known angelshark habitat, including minimum distance from shore restrictions and observer coverage.

actionItems.title: Support Marine Protected Areas
actionItems.description: The Canary Islands hosts critical angelshark habitat. Advocate for strengthened MPA enforcement there and the designation of new protected zones along the Iberian Atlantic coast and in the western Mediterranean where remnant populations may persist.

actionItems.title: Avoid Purchasing Shark Products
actionItems.description: Angelshark meat has historically been sold in Mediterranean markets. Support sustainable seafood campaigns and choose certified fisheries products to reduce demand driving incidental shark mortality.

healthMetrics.label: IUCN Status
healthMetrics.value: Critically Endangered (uplisted from Vulnerable in 2006; reassessed 2019)

healthMetrics.label: Population Trend
healthMetrics.value: Declining; functionally extinct across most of historic range in NE Atlantic and Mediterranean

healthMetrics.label: Habitat Quality
healthMetrics.value: Degraded — coastal benthic habitats heavily impacted by trawling and development

healthMetrics.label: Prey Availability
healthMetrics.value: Reduced — flatfish and crustacean stocks heavily fished across range

healthMetrics.label: Fishing Pressure
healthMetrics.value: High — bycatch mortality continues in trawl and gillnet fisheries despite protections

healthMetrics.label: Protection Coverage
healthMetrics.value: Improving — protected in UK (2008), EU waters, and several Mediterranean states; Canary Islands MPA coverage expanding

populationTrendMeta.note: No reliable global census exists. Figures are rough relative estimates modeled from documented regional collapses: the species is considered functionally extinct in the North Sea and most of the Mediterranean. The Canary Islands represents the last known viable stronghold. IUCN assessments note a suspected decline exceeding 80% over three generations (~75 years).

preyDeclineRegions.name: Mediterranean
preyDeclineRegions.note: Flatfish, rays, and benthic crustaceans severely depleted by generations of intensive demersal trawling

preyDeclineRegions.name: NE Atlantic
preyDeclineRegions.note: Sole, plaice, and other demersal prey species under sustained pressure from European fishing fleets

preyDeclineRegions.name: Canary Islands
preyDeclineRegions.note: Relatively productive reef and sandy shelf habitats; better prey availability supports the main surviving angelshark population

preyDeclineRegions.name: Iberian Atlantic
preyDeclineRegions.note: Coastal demersal fish stocks fished but some recovery noted in areas with improved management

fishingPressureRegions.name: Mediterranean
fishingPressureRegions.note: No effective directed prohibition in most member states; pervasive demersal trawling and gillnet effort throughout former range

fishingPressureRegions.name: NE Atlantic
fishingPressureRegions.note: EU zero-retention rule for angelsharks applies; ongoing bycatch and non-compliance documented; sparse observer coverage

fishingPressureRegions.name: Canary Islands
fishingPressureRegions.note: Protected; Angel Shark Project collaborates with local fishers on bycatch mitigation; pressure reduced but not eliminated

fishingPressureRegions.name: UK/Ireland
fishingPressureRegions.note: Legal protection since 2008 under UK Wildlife and Countryside Act; low encounter frequency given reduced population
